items.description:
Functional patterns in JavaScript are programming approaches based on using functions as the main building blocks. They enable writing more predictable, testable, and reusable code.

Callbacks:

Functions passed as arguments to other functions and called after an operation completes. This is the foundation of asynchronous programming in JavaScript.

IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression):

A function that executes immediately after declaration. Used to create an isolated scope and protect the global environment from pollution.

Callback Hell:

An anti-pattern that occurs with excessive nesting of callbacks. Leads to hard-to-read code that is difficult to maintain and debug.

Currying:

Transforming a function with multiple arguments into a sequence of functions, each taking a single argument. Allows creating specialized versions of functions.

Partial Application:

Fixing a subset of function arguments to create a new function that expects the remaining arguments. Simplifies code reuse.

Pure Functions:

Functions that have no side effects and always return the same result for the same arguments. Easy to test and predictable.

Function Composition:

Combining multiple simple functions into one complex function, where the result of each function is passed to the next.

Immutability:

The principle where data is not modified, but new copies are created with the desired changes. Ensures predictability.

Higher-Order Functions:

Functions that accept other functions as arguments or return functions as results. The foundation for map, filter, reduce.

Closures:

The ability of a function to remember and access variables from its outer scope even after that scope has finished executing.

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) in JavaScript is a paradigm based on using objects and classes to organize code. Core principles: encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction.

Classes:

Syntactic sugar over prototypes introduced in ES6. Allows creating objects with a unified structure.

Constructor:

A special method called when creating a class instance. Initializes object properties.

Class Fields:

Public fields are accessible from outside, private fields (denoted by #) are accessible only inside the class, static fields belong to the class itself.

Class Methods:

Functions defined inside a class. Can be instance, static, or private.

Inheritance:

A mechanism for creating a new class based on an existing one using the extends keyword.

super():

A keyword for calling the parent class constructor and methods.

Prototypal Inheritance:

JavaScript is based on prototypal inheritance. Each object has an internal [[Prototype]] reference to another prototype object.

__proto__:

A property pointing to the prototype object. Deprecated approach but commonly found in code.

prototype:

A property of constructor functions that defines the prototype for created objects.

Object.create():

A method that creates a new object with the specified prototype.

Factory Functions:

Functions that return new objects without using the new keyword.

Design Patterns:

Typical solutions for common problems. Main patterns: Singleton, Factory, Observer, Module, Mixin.

Encapsulation:

Hiding internal state of an object and providing access only through public methods.

Polymorphism:

The ability of objects of different classes to handle calls to the same method in their own way.

Prototypal inheritance is a mechanism in JavaScript where objects inherit properties and methods from other objects. Unlike classical OOP where inheritance comes from classes, in JavaScript objects inherit directly from other objects.

[[Prototype]]:

An internal hidden property of every object that points to its prototype. Not directly accessible in code.

__proto__:

A getter/setter for accessing the internal [[Prototype]]. Deprecated but widely used. Recommended to use Object.getPrototypeOf() and Object.setPrototypeOf().

prototype:

A property of constructor functions. Defines the prototype for all objects created with new using that function.

Object.getPrototypeOf():

A modern method for getting an object's prototype. Preferred over using __proto__.

Object.setPrototypeOf():

A method for setting an object's prototype. Use with caution due to performance implications.

Object.create():

A method that creates a new object with the specified prototype. Allows setting the prototype when creating an object.

Prototype Chain:

A property lookup mechanism: if a property is not found in the object, the search continues up the prototype chain to the topmost Object.prototype.

Constructor Functions:

Regular functions called with the new operator. Create objects and automatically set the prototype through the prototype property.

instanceof:

An operator that checks if an object is an instance of a given constructor function. Checks if the constructor exists in the prototype chain.

hasOwnProperty():

A method that checks if an object has its own property (not from the prototype chain).

Object.hasOwn():

A modern alternative to hasOwnProperty(). A static Object method.

Object.keys() / Object.values() / Object.entries():

Methods that return only the object's own enumerable properties, ignoring the prototype.

for...in:

A loop that iterates over all enumerable properties of an object, including those in the prototype chain.

Inheritance through Object.create():

Creating objects with an explicit prototype without using constructor functions and new.

Classes (ES6):

Syntactic sugar over prototypal inheritance. Classes hide prototype implementation details but use the same underlying mechanism.

Mixins:

A pattern for multiple inheritance in JavaScript where one object borrows methods from another through Object.assign or property copying.

Scope in JavaScript determines the accessibility of variables and functions in different parts of code. Understanding scopes is critical for writing safe and predictable code.

Global Scope:

Variables declared outside any functions or blocks are in the global scope. Accessible from anywhere in the code, including functions and blocks. Overusing global variables can lead to naming conflicts and debugging difficulties.

Function Scope:

Variables declared inside a function using var are accessible only within that function. They are not visible outside it. Each function creates its own scope.

Block Scope:

Variables declared with let and const inside a block {} (if, for, while, etc.) are accessible only within that block. This is more predictable behavior than var.

var:

Declares a variable with function scope. Ignores block scope, hoisted to the top of the function. Not recommended for modern code.

let:

Declares a variable with block scope. Not fully hoisted, exists in a temporal dead zone until declaration. The preferred way to declare mutable variables.

const:

Declares a constant with block scope. Cannot be reassigned. Objects declared with const can have their properties modified but cannot be reassigned.

Hoisting:

A mechanism where variable and function declarations are moved to the top of their scope during compilation. var is hoisted with undefined, let and const are hoisted without initialization, functions are fully hoisted.

Temporal Dead Zone:

The period between entering a scope and the actual declaration of a let or const variable, when the variable exists but cannot be used. Accessing it causes a ReferenceError.

Lexical Scope:

Scope determined by the position of code in the source text. Inner functions have access to variables of outer functions.

Closures:

The ability of a function to remember its lexical scope even after the outer function has executed. Allows creating private variables and functions.

IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression):

A function that executes immediately after creation. Creates an isolated scope to protect against polluting the global context.

Modules:

A modern way to isolate code. Each module has its own scope, exporting only what is necessary.

Global Object:

In browsers — window, in Node.js — global. Properties added to the global object become global variables.

strict mode:

A mode that prohibits implicit creation of global variables. Recommended to always use to prevent accidental global variables.

Best Practices:

1. Use const by default, let when reassignment is needed
2. Avoid var in new code
3. Minimize the number of global variables
4. Use modules to isolate code
5. Always declare variables before using them
6. Use strict mode ('use strict')

Scope in JavaScript determines where variables and functions are accessible in code. Understanding scopes helps avoid errors and write more predictable code.

Global Scope:

Variables declared outside any functions or blocks are in the global scope. Accessible from anywhere in the program. Overusing global variables can lead to naming conflicts and unexpected behavior.

Function Scope:

Variables declared with var inside a function are accessible only within that function. They are not visible outside it. Each function creates its own scope.

Block Scope:

Variables declared with let and const inside a block {} (if, for, while, switch) are accessible only within that block. This is more predictable behavior compared to var.

var:

Declares a variable with function scope. Ignores block constructs. Hoisted to the top of the function with undefined value. Not recommended for modern code.

let:

Declares a variable with block scope. Hoisted but remains in a temporal dead zone until declaration. Recommended for variables whose value may change.

const:

Declares a constant with block scope. Cannot be reassigned. Requires initialization at declaration. Objects and arrays declared with const can have their contents modified.

Hoisting:

A mechanism where variable and function declarations are moved to the top of their scope during compilation. var is hoisted with undefined, let and const are hoisted without initialization, function declarations are fully hoisted.

Temporal Dead Zone:

The period from entering a scope until the declaration of a let or const variable, when the variable exists but is inaccessible. Access causes a ReferenceError.

Lexical Scope:

Scope determined by the position of code in the source text. Inner functions have access to variables of outer functions.

Closures:

The ability of a function to remember its lexical scope even after the outer function has executed. Used to create private variables and functions.

IIFE (Immediately Invoked Function Expression):

A function that executes immediately after creation. Creates an isolated scope to protect against polluting the global context.

Modules:

A modern way to isolate code. Each module has its own scope, exporting only what is necessary.

strict mode:

A mode that prohibits implicit creation of global variables. Recommended to always use to prevent accidental global variables.

Best Practices:

1. Use const by default, let when reassignment is needed
2. Avoid var in new code
3. Minimize the number of global variables
4. Use modules to isolate code
5. Always declare variables before using them
6. Use strict mode ('use strict')

Nested scopes in JavaScript occur when one function or block is declared inside another function or block. Inner scopes have access to variables of outer scopes, but not vice versa.

Scope Chain:

A mechanism where JavaScript searches for a variable first in the current scope, then in the outer scope, and so on up to the global scope. If the variable is not found, a ReferenceError occurs.

Access to Outer Variables:

Inner functions have access to all variables of outer functions, including parameters and local variables. This is the foundation for closures and lexical scoping.

Variable Lookup:

When accessing a variable, JavaScript checks the current scope, then moves up the scope chain to the global scope. The first found value is used.

Variable Shadowing:

When a variable in an inner scope has the same name as a variable in an outer scope, the inner variable shadows the outer one. Access to the outer variable becomes impossible within that block.

Nested Functions:

Functions defined inside other functions. They form a scope chain and have access to variables of the parent function.

Blocks Inside Functions:

Blocks (if, for, while) inside functions create their own block scope for let and const. var ignores block scopes and remains in the function scope.

Multiple Nesting:

Scopes can be nested to any depth. Each inner scope has access to all outer scopes up the chain.

Closures:

A special case of nested scopes where an inner function retains access to variables of an outer function even after it has finished executing. Used to create private variables and functions.

Lexical Environment:

An internal structure that stores variables and a reference to the outer environment. Each function and block has its own lexical environment.

Loss of Context (this):

Nested functions create their own this context. To access the this of the outer function, use arrow functions, bind, call, apply, or save to a variable.

Arrow Functions:

Do not create their own this and arguments scope, but inherit them from the outer function. Simplify working with nested functions.

Dangers of Nested Scopes:

Creating too deep nesting can lead to performance issues and code readability problems. It is recommended to extract complex logic into separate functions.

Best Practices:

1. Avoid excessive nesting (no more than 3-4 levels)
2. Use arrow functions to preserve this context
3. Be careful with variable shadowing
4. Extract deeply nested functions into separate modules
5. Use let and const instead of var for predictable block behavior
6. Understand the difference between function and block scope

Function parameters are variables that receive values passed when a function is called. Proper definition and use of parameters makes functions flexible and reusable.

Required Parameters:

Parameters that must be passed when calling a function. If no value is passed, the parameter receives undefined. Can check for presence and throw an error.

Default Parameters:

Allow setting a default value if an argument is not passed or is undefined. Specified using an equals sign in the parameter definition.

Rest Parameters:

Collect all remaining arguments into an array. Denoted by three dots before the parameter (...args). Must be the last in the parameter list.

Parameter Destructuring:

Allows extracting values from objects and arrays directly in function parameters. Simplifies working with complex data structures.

Object Parameters:

Passing a single object instead of multiple parameters. Simplifies function calls and makes code more readable. Often combined with destructuring and default parameters.

arguments (deprecated):

An object available inside regular functions (not arrow functions) containing all passed arguments. Not an array, requires conversion. Replaced by rest parameters in modern code.

Arrow Function Parameters:

Arrow functions support all parameter definition methods. Do not have their own arguments object, use parameters from the outer scope.

Parameter Order:

Recommended order: required parameters first, then default parameters, then rest parameters. Improves readability and predictability.

Pass by Value vs Pass by Reference:

Primitive types (numbers, strings, booleans) are passed by value — changes inside the function do not affect the original variable. Objects and arrays are passed by reference — changes inside the function affect the original object.

Parameter Validation:

Important to check parameter types and values, especially in public functions. Can use TypeScript, JSDoc, or manual checking.

Optional Parameters:

Parameters that can be omitted. Usually implemented through default parameters or checking for undefined.

Best Practices:

1. Use default parameters instead of checking for undefined
2. Use destructuring for working with objects and arrays
3. Use rest parameters instead of arguments
4. Limit the number of parameters (3-4, otherwise use an object)
5. Give parameters meaningful names
6. Use object parameters for complex configurations
7. Be careful when passing objects by reference
8. Add parameter validation for public functions

items.value: Differences in parameters passing by value and by reference
items.description:
In JavaScript, function parameters can be passed in two ways: by value (for primitive types) and by reference (for objects and arrays). Understanding this difference is critical for predictable code behavior.

Pass by Value:

Primitive types (string, number, boolean, null, undefined, symbol, bigint) are passed by value. The function receives a copy of the value. Changes inside the function do not affect the original variable. Each variable stores its own independent value.

Pass by Reference:

Objects and arrays are passed by reference. The function receives a reference to the same object in memory. Changes inside the function affect the original object. Multiple variables can reference the same object.

Primitive Types:

Immutable. Any operation creates a new value. When passed to a function, an independent copy is created. Changing the parameter does not affect the outer variable.

Objects and Arrays:

Mutable. A variable stores a reference to the object, not the object itself. When passed to a function, the reference is copied, but both parameters point to the same object. Changing object properties affects the original object.

Assignment:

When assigning a primitive, a new copy of the value is created. When assigning an object, the reference is copied, both objects point to the same memory location.

Copying Objects:

To create an independent copy of an object, use: spread operator (...), Object.assign(), structured cloning (structuredClone), or libraries for deep copying.

Deep vs Shallow Copy:

Shallow copying creates a new object, but nested objects remain shared. Deep copying creates a completely independent copy of all nesting levels.

Immutability:

An approach where objects are not modified, but new copies are created with the desired changes. Used in functional programming and many modern frameworks.

Pure Functions:

Functions that do not modify passed objects but return new ones. Easier to test and predictable.

Dangers of Pass by Reference:

Unexpected object modification in different parts of the program. Difficulty tracking where and when an object was modified. Issues with asynchronous code.

Practical Examples:

Primitives: changes inside the function do not affect the outer variable. Objects: property changes inside the function affect the original object. Complete object replacement inside the function does not affect the outer reference.

Best Practices:

1. Do not modify passed objects unless expected
2. Create copies of objects before making changes
3. Use spread operator for shallow copying
4. Use structuredClone for deep copying
5. Prefer immutable data structures
6. Document if a function modifies passed objects
7. Use TypeScript to control types and prevent unexpected mutations

items.value: Handling a dynamic amount of function parameters.
items.description:
In JavaScript, functions can accept a variable number of arguments. This allows creating flexible APIs, wrapper functions, and working with data collections without being tied to a fixed number of parameters.

Rest Parameters:

The ...args syntax collects all passed arguments into an array. Must be the last parameter in the list. Works with any number of arguments. The preferred approach in modern JavaScript.

arguments Object (deprecated):

Available inside regular functions (not arrow functions). Contains all passed arguments. Array-like but does not have array methods. Requires conversion to an array to use array methods. Replaced by rest parameters in modern code.

Converting arguments to an array:

To work with array methods, arguments must be converted: Array.from(arguments), [...arguments], or Array.prototype.slice.call(arguments).

Function.length:

The length property shows the number of declared parameters of a function. Does not count rest parameters. Useful for introspection and creating adaptive functions.

Function Overloading:

JavaScript does not support classical function overloading. Emulated by checking the number and types of arguments inside a single function.

Checking Argument Count:

Can check arguments.length or rest parameters to determine passed arguments. Allows implementing different behavior depending on the number of parameters.

Array Destructuring:

Allows processing an array of arguments by extracting needed elements. Convenient for functions working with collections.

Function Factories:

Functions that return new functions with a fixed number of parameters based on passed arguments. Used to create specialized versions.

Function Adapters:

Wrapper functions that transform calls with different numbers of arguments. Examples: debounce, throttle, once, memoize.

Partial Application:

Fixing part of a function's arguments using bind or closures. Allows creating new functions with fewer parameters.

Currying:

Transforming a function with multiple arguments into a sequence of functions with a single argument. Simplifies partial application and composition.

Arguments in Arrow Functions:

Arrow functions do not have their own arguments object. Use rest parameters or refer to the outer function's arguments to work with dynamic arguments.

Best Practices:

1. Use rest parameters instead of arguments
2. Limit the number of parameters when possible
3. Use an object parameter for multiple options
4. Document the expected number of arguments
5. Check for required arguments
6. Use default parameters for optional values
7. Use destructuring to work with argument arrays
8. Avoid excessive complexity in argument handling

KISS is a design principle that advocates simplicity as a key factor in code reliability and maintainability. Complex solutions are more prone to errors and harder to maintain.

Core Idea:

Code should be as simple and understandable as possible. Any complexity must be justified by real requirements. Simple code is easier to read, test, debug, and modify.

Signs of Violation:

Excessive abstraction, premature optimization, unnecessary nesting levels, complex conditions, magic numbers, code duplication, functions that do too much.

Simplicity vs Naivety:

Simplicity is the result of conscious choice, not lack of knowledge. Simple code uses clear constructs and avoids unnecessary complexity. Naive code fails to account for real requirements.

Practical Rules:

Write code that can be understood on first reading. Use meaningful variable and function names. Break complex tasks into simple steps. Avoid deep nesting. One function — one responsibility.

Avoid Premature Optimization:

Optimize only when there is a real performance problem. Clean and understandable code is more important than micro-optimizations. Modern JavaScript engines optimize simple code well.

Managing Complexity:

Complexity is inevitable but can be managed: split into modules, use composition over inheritance, extract complex logic into separate functions, document complex areas.

Refactoring:

Regularly review and simplify code. Remove unused code. Consolidate duplicate logic. Rewrite complex areas in a simpler way.

Comments:

Comments should not explain what the code does — that should be clear from the code itself. Comments explain why a particular approach was chosen when it's not obvious.

Testing:

Simple code is easier to test. Fewer mocks, fewer dependencies, simpler test setup. Each test should verify one thing.

Relationship with Other Principles:

KISS works together with DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It), SOLID. Simplicity often results from following these principles.

Best Practices:

1. Write code for humans, not for computers
2. Use meaningful variable and function names
3. Break large functions into smaller ones
4. Limit nesting (3-4 levels maximum)
5. Avoid clever but obscure constructs
6. Remove dead and unused code
7. Refactor complex areas
8. Ask yourself: can this be simpler?

DRY is a software development principle aimed at eliminating repetitive code. Every piece of knowledge should have a single, unambiguous representation within a system.

Core Idea:

Any logic, knowledge, or rule should exist in the code only once. Repetition leads to duplication, which increases maintenance costs and the risk of errors.

Types of Repetition:

Code duplication — identical code blocks in different places. Logic duplication — same algorithms with different implementations. Data duplication — same information stored in multiple places. Knowledge duplication — same business rule expressed differently.

Problems with Duplication:

When changing logic, code must be modified in multiple places. High risk of missing one location. Increased code volume. Harder to read and understand. Testing requires more effort.

Ways to Eliminate Repetition:

Extract to functions — repetitive operations are moved to a separate function. Extract to modules — common logic is moved to separate modules. Inheritance — common logic is moved to a base class. Composition — common behavior is implemented through separate objects.

Abstraction:

Creating proper abstractions helps eliminate duplication. Abstraction should reflect essence, not implementation details. Premature abstraction can complicate code.

Single Responsibility Principle:

Each function, class, or module should be responsible for one thing. This naturally helps eliminate duplication.

Configuration Instead of Copying:

If code differs only in parameters, use configuration rather than copying. Parameterization allows one function to handle different cases.

Copy-Paste:

Copying code is the main enemy of DRY. Each copy-paste creates future problems. Instead of copying, find a way to extract common logic.

When Repetition is Acceptable:

Different application layers may have similar data structures (DTOs and domain models). Different contexts may have independent implementations. Sometimes repetition for readability may be justified.

DRY and Performance:

Eliminating duplication may slightly affect performance. In most cases, readability and maintainability are more important than micro-optimizations.

DRY and Testing:

Eliminating duplication simplifies testing. It's enough to cover one implementation with tests rather than multiple copies. Fewer tests — less maintenance.

Best Practices:

1. Extract repetitive code into functions
2. Create modules for common logic
3. Use parameterization instead of copying
4. Apply inheritance and composition
5. Avoid copy-paste
6. Refactor when discovering repetitions
7. Create proper abstractions
8. Remember: knowledge duplication is more dangerous than code duplication

YAGNI is a software development principle that warns against adding functionality "just in case." Implement only what you need now, not what you might need in the future.

Core Idea:

Don't write code that is not required at the current moment. It's hard to predict what will actually be needed in the future. Extra functionality increases complexity and maintenance costs.

Problems with Premature Implementation:

Wasting time on unnecessary features. Complicating the codebase. Increasing test volume. Making code harder to understand. Risk of bugs in unused code. Difficulty with refactoring.

Typical Violations:

Adding parameters "for future use." Creating universal functions "for all cases." Implementing flexibility that is not needed. Premature optimization. Complex abstractions for simple tasks.

Premature Optimization:

Optimizing code before real performance issues arise. Often leads to code complexity without measurable benefit. Optimize only after measuring and identifying bottlenecks.

Premature Abstraction:

Creating interfaces, base classes, and abstractions before a real need emerges. Abstractions should emerge during refactoring when actual duplication occurs.

Flexibility for the Future:

Attempts to make code maximally flexible "just in case." Often leads to over-engineering. Real flexibility comes from concrete requirements, not assumptions.

When Violation is Justified:

Clearly known future requirements. Standard libraries and utilities. Infrastructure code. Experimental prototypes. Documented technical debt.

Relationship with Other Principles:

YAGNI complements KISS (don't complicate) and DRY (but don't create abstractions until repetition appears). Works together with the minimum viable product (MVP) principle.

Refactoring Over Foresight:

It's easier to add functionality later than to remove unnecessary code. Refactoring allows adapting code to new requirements. Trust refactoring, not foresight.

Economic Benefit:

Writing unnecessary code is a direct waste of time and money. Every unused line of code requires maintenance. Removing code is harder than writing it.

Best Practices:

1. Implement only current requirements
2. Don't add parameters and options "for the future"
3. Create abstractions only when truly needed
4. Optimize only after measurement
5. Remove unused code
6. Ask: is this needed now or just seems useful?
7. Trust refactoring, not foresight
8. Remember: every extra line of code is a potential bug

this in JavaScript is a special keyword that refers to the object in whose context the current code is executed. Unlike other languages, the value of this is determined not at declaration time, but at function call time.

Global Context:

In the global scope (outside any functions), this refers to the global object. In browsers it's window, in Node.js it's global, in strict mode it's undefined.

Function Context:

The value of this inside a regular function depends on how the function was called. In non-strict mode with a regular call, this refers to the global object. In strict mode — undefined.

Object Methods:

When a function is called as an object method, this refers to the object that owns the method. This is one of the most common and understandable cases of using this.

Constructors and new:

When a function is called with the new operator, this refers to the newly created object. The constructor uses this to initialize properties of the new instance.

Arrow Functions:

Arrow functions do not have their own this. They inherit this from the outer lexical scope. The value of this is determined at declaration time, not at call time.

Explicit Binding:

The call, apply, and bind methods allow explicitly setting the value of this for a function. call and apply invoke the function immediately, bind returns a new function with bound this.

Loss of Context:

When passing a method as a callback, this loses its reference to the original object. To preserve context, use bind, arrow functions, or save the reference to a variable.

this in Event Handlers:

In DOM event handlers, this refers to the element to which the handler is attached. In arrow functions — inherits context.

this in Classes:

In classes, this refers to the class instance. Class methods are not bound to the instance by default, requiring explicit binding in the constructor or using arrow functions.

this in Nested Functions:

In regular functions nested inside a method, this loses the outer object's context. Solution — save this to an outer variable (self, that) or use an arrow function.

Method Binding:

To pass class methods to callbacks, binding is required: bind in the constructor, arrow functions as class fields, or using arrow functions at the call site.

Best Practices:

1. Use arrow functions to preserve context
2. Use bind for explicit this binding
3. Save this to a variable (const self = this) in nested functions
4. In classes, bind methods in the constructor
5. Avoid passing unbound methods to callbacks
6. Use call and apply to invoke functions with the desired context
7. Remember that arrow functions do not have their own this
8. In strict mode ('use strict'), this behavior is more predictable

Loss of context in JavaScript occurs when a function that is an object method is called outside of its association with that object. As a result, this inside the function no longer refers to the original object.

Causes of Context Loss:

Method passed as a callback. Method stored in a variable. Method passed to setTimeout or setInterval. Method used as an event handler. Nested functions inside a method.

Passing Method as Callback:

When a method is passed as an argument to another function, it loses its connection to the original object. The function calls the method as a regular function, without binding to the object.

Storing Method in Variable:

Assigning a method to a variable breaks the connection with the object. Calling via the variable lacks object context.

setTimeout and setInterval:

Passing a method to timers causes context loss because the method is called as a regular function after the time elapses.

Event Handlers:

When passing a method to an event handler, this starts referring to the DOM element rather than the original object.

Nested Functions:

Inside a method, a regular function creates its own this context, which does not inherit the this of the outer method.

Solutions:

Arrow functions do not have their own this and inherit it from the outer scope. bind creates a new function with bound this. Saving this to a variable (self, that) for use in nested functions. Explicit binding in class constructor.

Arrow Functions:

The simplest and most modern way to preserve context. Use arrow functions instead of regular functions in nested functions and callbacks.

bind Method:

Creates a new function with a bound this value. Allows passing methods to other functions while preserving context.

Saving to a Variable:

Classic pattern const self = this or const that = this. Saves a reference to this in an outer variable for use in nested functions.

Binding in Constructor:

In classes, methods can be bound to the instance directly in the constructor: this.method = this.method.bind(this).

Class Fields with Arrow Functions:

Modern approach: declare class methods as arrow functions. They are automatically bound to the instance.

Best Practices:

1. Use arrow functions for callbacks
2. Use bind for passing methods
3. In classes, bind methods in constructor or use arrow fields
4. Avoid passing unbound methods to timers and handlers
5. Save this to a variable in nested functions
6. Use modern class syntax with arrow methods
7. Remember: context loss is a common error, be careful
8. Test methods that are passed to other functions

Explicit binding in JavaScript is the ability to forcibly set the this value for a function using the call, apply, and bind methods. This allows controlling the execution context of a function regardless of how it was called.

call Method:

Calls a function with a specified this value and arguments passed individually. First argument is the this value, the rest are function arguments. The function executes immediately.

apply Method:

Calls a function with a specified this value and arguments passed as an array. First argument is the this value, second is an array of arguments. Useful when the number of arguments is unknown or they are already in an array.

bind Method:

Creates a new function with a bound this value. The original function is not called, but a new function with fixed this is returned. Allows creating functions with preset context for later invocation.

Differences Between call, apply, and bind:

call and apply invoke the function immediately, bind returns a new function. call takes arguments as a list, apply as an array. bind allows creating partially applied functions with fixed arguments.

Binding Method Context:

Often used to bind class methods to the instance in the constructor. Ensures that when a method is passed as a callback, this refers to the correct object.

Partial Application:

bind allows fixing not only this but also some of the function's arguments. Creates a new function with preset initial arguments.

Currying:

Explicit binding is used to implement currying — transforming a function with multiple arguments into a sequence of functions with a single argument.

Method Borrowing:

Using call and apply, you can borrow methods from one object to use with another object. For example, array methods can be applied to array-like objects.

Binding in Event Handlers:

bind is used to bind this in event handlers so that a class method has access to the instance rather than the DOM element.

Binding in Timers:

setTimeout and setInterval lose context. bind allows binding the desired this before passing the method to a timer.

Hard Binding:

Creating a function with permanently fixed this. Even when trying to override this with call or apply, the bound value remains unchanged.

Arrow Functions:

Arrow functions do not have their own this, so call, apply, and bind cannot change their this. They always use this from the outer lexical scope.

null and undefined as this:

When passing null or undefined to call, apply, or bind, this in non-strict mode is replaced with the global object. In strict mode, it remains null or undefined.

Best Practices:

1. Use bind to create functions with fixed context
2. Use call and apply for immediate invocation with desired this
3. Use apply when working with arrays of arguments
4. Bind class methods in the constructor
5. Remember that arrow functions do not support explicit binding
6. Use strict mode for predictable behavior
7. Avoid overusing explicit binding where lexical binding works
8. Method borrowing via call and apply is powerful but use with caution

Arrow functions in JavaScript are a concise syntax for writing functions that have a key difference: they do not create their own this context, but inherit it from the outer lexical scope.

No Own this:

Arrow functions do not have their own this. The value of this inside an arrow function is determined at declaration time and equals the this of the outer function or global context.

Lexical Binding:

this in an arrow function is bound to the context where the function was defined, not where it is called. This makes this behavior predictable and eliminates the need for bind or saving to a variable.

Advantages of Arrow Functions:

Automatic context preservation. Simplified work with callbacks. No this loss in nested functions. Cleaner and more concise code.

Arrow Functions and Object Methods:

Using arrow functions as object methods can lead to unexpected behavior because this will refer to the outer scope, not the object.

Arrow Functions in Classes:

Class fields with arrow functions automatically bind the method to the class instance. This is a convenient alternative to binding in the constructor.

Arrow Functions and call/apply/bind:

Since arrow functions do not have their own this, the call, apply, and bind methods cannot change it. Attempting to bind a different this will be ignored.

Arrow Functions in Event Handlers:

In DOM event handlers, arrow functions inherit this from the outer scope rather than referencing the element. This can be both useful and problematic.

Arrow Functions and Constructors:

Arrow functions cannot be used as constructors with the new operator. They do not have a prototype property and cannot create objects this way.

Arrow Functions and arguments Object:

Arrow functions do not have their own arguments object. Rest parameters (...args) are used to access arguments.

When to Use Arrow Functions:

Callbacks in map, filter, reduce methods. Nested functions inside methods. Timers (setTimeout, setInterval). Promises and asynchronous code. Class methods for automatic binding.

When Not to Use Arrow Functions:

Object methods that need access to the object via this. Constructors. Functions that need their own arguments object. Functions where this should be determined dynamically.

Comparison with Regular Functions:

Regular functions have their own this, determined at call time. Arrow functions inherit this from the declaration location. Regular functions can be used as constructors, arrow functions cannot.

Best Practices:

1. Use arrow functions for callbacks and nested functions
2. Use regular functions for object methods
3. Use arrow class fields for automatic binding
4. Do not try to change arrow function this with call/apply/bind
5. Use rest parameters instead of arguments
6. Remember that arrow functions are not suitable for constructors
7. Use arrow functions to preserve context in timers and handlers
8. Be consistent in choosing function types in your project

Stack is a data structure that operates on the LIFO principle (Last In, First Out).

Basic Operations:

push — add an element to the top of the stack
pop — remove an element from the top of the stack
peek — view the top element without removing

How It Works:

Elements are added and removed only from one end (the top). The last element added will be the first one removed.

Example in JavaScript:

const stack = [];
stack.push(1); // [1]
stack.push(2); // [1, 2]
stack.push(3); // [1, 2, 3]
stack.pop(); // 3, stack becomes [1, 2]

Applications in Programming:

Function call management (call stack). Expression parsing and syntax analysis. Handling nested structures. Undo/redo operations. Depth-first traversal of trees and graphs.

Characteristics:

Time complexity O(1) for push, pop, peek. Simple implementation using arrays or linked lists. Restricted access — only to the top.

items.value: Data Types: Set, Map, WeakSet, WeakMap
items.description:
Set — a collection of unique values of any type. Values are stored without keys, each value appears only once.

Set Operations:

new Set() — create a collection
add(value) — add a value
has(value) — check existence
delete(value) — delete a value
clear() — clear the collection
size — number of elements

Set Example:

const set = new Set([1, 2, 3, 3, 4]);
set.add(5);
set.has(2); // true
set.delete(3);

Map — a key-value collection where keys can be of any type (objects, functions, primitives). Maintains insertion order.

Map Operations:

new Map() — create a collection
set(key, value) — add/update
get(key) — get a value
has(key) — check existence
delete(key) — delete
clear() — clear
size — number of elements

Map Example:

const map = new Map();
map.set('name', 'John');
map.set({ id: 1 }, 'user');
map.get('name'); // John

WeakSet — a collection of unique objects, stores only weak references. Objects can be garbage collected if no other references exist.

WeakSet Features:

Objects only (no primitives). Not enumerable (no size, forEach). Automatic object removal on garbage collection. Cannot iterate.

WeakSet Example:

const weakSet = new WeakSet();
const obj = { id: 1 };
weakSet.add(obj);
weakSet.has(obj); // true
obj = null; // object will be removed from WeakSet

WeakMap — a key-value collection with weak references to keys. Keys are objects only. Values can be any type.

WeakMap Features:

Keys are objects only. Weak references to keys. Not enumerable (no size, forEach). Automatic entry removal when key is garbage collected.

WeakMap Example:

const weakMap = new WeakMap();
const user = { id: 1 };
weakMap.set(user, 'user data');
weakMap.get(user); // 'user data'
user = null; // entry will be removed from WeakMap

Comparison:

Set — unique values, any types. Map — key-value, any keys. WeakSet — objects only, weak references. WeakMap — object keys, weak references.

Applications:

Set — remove duplicates, check uniqueness. Map — store data with any keys, caching. WeakSet — track objects without memory leaks. WeakMap — store private data, metadata for objects.

Asynchrony in JavaScript — executing operations without blocking the main thread. Based on Event Loop, microtasks, and macrotasks.

Event Loop:

Continuously checks the task queue. Executes synchronous code first, then microtasks, then macrotasks.

Microtasks: Promise.then, async/await, MutationObserver, queueMicrotask
Macrotasks: setTimeout, setInterval, I/O, DOM events

Garbage Collection:

Automatic memory deallocation for objects with no references.

Garbage Collection Algorithms:

Mark-and-Sweep — marks reachable objects, removes unreachable ones. Used in modern engines.

Memory Leaks:

Global variables. Unremoved event handlers. Closures with unnecessary references. Uncleared timers.

WeakMap and WeakSet:

Prevent memory leaks. Store weak references to objects. Objects can be garbage collected.

Connection Between Asynchrony and GC:

Async operations may hold references in callbacks. Uncleared timers and handlers can cause leaks. WeakMap are useful for caching in async code.

Best Practices:

Clear timers (clearTimeout, clearInterval). Remove event handlers. Avoid global variables. Use WeakMap for metadata storage. Avoid creating infinite promises without handling.

Window — the global browser object representing a window or tab. It is the root object for all global variables and browser APIs.

Main Properties:

window.document — access to DOM
document — shorthand notation
window.innerWidth/innerHeight — viewport dimensions
window.outerWidth/outerHeight — browser window dimensions
window.location — URL information
window.history — history management
window.localStorage/sessionStorage — data storage
window.navigator — browser information
window.screen — screen information

Page Lifecycle:

Loading Stages:

1. DOMContentLoaded — DOM is built, elements can be accessed. Async and deferred scripts may still be loading.

2. load — all resources (images, styles, iframes) are fully loaded.

3. beforeunload — before closing the page. Can show a warning.

4. unload — page is unloading. Not recommended for use.

Lifecycle Events:

document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {});
window.addEventListener('load', () => {});
window.addEventListener('beforeunload', (e) => {
  e.preventDefault();
  e.returnValue = '';
});
window.addEventListener('unload', () => {});

Page Visibility States:

document.hidden — whether page is hidden
document.visibilityState — 'visible', 'hidden', 'prerender'
visibilitychange — visibility change event

Scroll and Dimensions:

window.scrollX, window.scrollY — scroll position
window.scrollTo(x, y) — scroll to position
window.scrollBy(x, y) — scroll relative

Opening and Closing Windows:

window.open(url) — open a new window
window.close() — close a window
window.opener — reference to the parent window

Security:

window.postMessage — cross-window messaging
window.opener = null — break connection when opening

Best Practices:

Use DOMContentLoaded for DOM initialization. Use load for working with images. Do not use unload for critical operations. Clean up event handlers when leaving the page.

Network requests and timers are the foundation of asynchronous programming in the browser. Both mechanisms use the Event Loop.

Timers:

setTimeout(fn, delay) — single delay
setInterval(fn, delay) — periodic execution
requestAnimationFrame(fn) — sync with rendering

Timer Cleanup:

clearTimeout(id)
clearInterval(id)
cancelAnimationFrame(id)

Network Requests:

fetch(url, options) — modern API for HTTP requests

fetch('/api/data')
  .then(response => response.json())
  .catch(error => console.error(error));

XMLHttpRequest — legacy, but still in use

Abstractions:

async/await — syntactic sugar for promises
try {
  const response = await fetch('/api/data');
  const data = await response.json();
} catch (error) {
  console.error(error);
}

fetch Options:

method — GET, POST, PUT, DELETE
headers — request headers
body — request body (JSON, FormData)
credentials — send cookies (include, same-origin)

Features:

Timers do not guarantee exact execution time. fetch does not reject promises on HTTP errors (404, 500) — only on network errors. AbortController is used to cancel requests.

Best Practices:

Always clear timers when leaving the page. Handle network request errors. Use async/await for readability. Cancel requests when unmounting components.

Design patterns are typical solutions to common problems. SOLID are five principles of object-oriented design.

Creational Patterns:

Singleton — ensures only one instance of a class exists
Factory — creates objects without specifying the concrete class
Builder — step-by-step construction of complex objects

Structural Patterns:

Adapter — converts one class interface to another
Decorator — adds functionality to an object
Proxy — placeholder for controlling access

Behavioral Patterns:

Observer — notifies dependent objects about changes
Strategy — selects algorithm at runtime
Module — encapsulates code in modules (IIFE, ES6 modules)

SOLID:

S — Single Responsibility. A class should have one reason to change.

O — Open/Closed. Open for extension, closed for modification.

L — Liskov Substitution. Objects can be replaced with subclass objects without breaking the program.

I — Interface Segregation. Many specific interfaces are better than one general interface.

D — Dependency Inversion. Depend on abstractions, not on concrete implementations.

Application in JavaScript:

Modules — Module pattern implementation. Promises and events — Observer pattern. Class-based inheritance — OOP inheritance. Higher-order functions — Strategy pattern. Middleware — Chain of Responsibility.

Best Practices:

Do not apply patterns unnecessarily. Patterns are tools, not goals. Follow SOLID for maintainable code. Use composition over inheritance. Write code that is easy to test.

Development methodologies — approaches to organizing the software creation process. Testing — ensuring code quality and reliability.

Development Methodologies:

Agile — flexible methodology, iterative development. Scrum — work in sprints, roles: Product Owner, Scrum Master, Team. Kanban — task flow visualization, WIP limits. Waterfall — cascade model, sequential phases.

Testing Types:

Unit — testing individual functions and classes. Integration — checking module interactions. E2E — testing user scenarios. Snapshot — comparison with reference (UI).

Test Types:

Unit tests — fast, many, cover logic. Integration tests — slower, check connections. E2E — slow, few, cover critical paths.

Testing Tools:

Jest — framework for unit tests (React, Node.js). Vitest — modern Jest alternative. Testing Library — component testing via user actions. Cypress, Playwright — E2E testing. Mock Service Worker — request interception.

Testing Principles:

AAA — Arrange, Act, Assert. TDD — test first, code later. Tests should be independent. One test — one assertion.

Metrics:

Code coverage — percentage of code covered by tests. Should not be a goal in itself; quality matters more.

Best Practices:

Write tests alongside code. Test behavior, not implementation. Use mocks for isolation. Keep tests clean. Automate test runs in CI/CD. Don't chase 100% coverage.

Web protocols are rules for data transmission on the internet. REST is an architectural style for building APIs.

HTTP/HTTPS:

HTTP — Hypertext Transfer Protocol
HTTPS — secure version with encryption (SSL/TLS)

HTTP Methods:

GET — retrieve data
POST — create resource
PUT — full update
PATCH — partial update
DELETE — delete

HTTP Statuses:

2xx — success (200 OK, 201 Created)
3xx — redirection (301 Moved Permanently)
4xx — client error (400 Bad Request, 404 Not Found)
5xx — server error (500 Internal Server Error)

REST (Representational State Transfer):

Resources — all data represented as resources (URLs). HTTP methods — for operations on resources. Statuses — for result indication. Stateless — server does not store client state.

REST Principles:

Client-server architecture. Stateless. Cacheability. Uniform interface.

URL Structure:

GET /users — list of users
GET /users/1 — specific user
POST /users — create
PUT /users/1 — update
DELETE /users/1 — delete

JSON — primary data exchange format.

WebSocket:

Bidirectional real-time communication. Persistent connection. Used for chats, games, notifications.

Web application security — protection against attacks and vulnerabilities. Understanding key threats is critical for development.

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting):

Injecting malicious script into a page. Types: reflected, stored, DOM-based. Protection: output escaping, Content Security Policy (CSP), use textContent instead of innerHTML.

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery):

Forcing a user to perform unwanted actions. Protection: CSRF tokens, SameSite cookies, Referer validation.

SQL Injection:

Injecting SQL code into database queries. Protection: parameterized queries, ORM, input validation.

Clickjacking:

Embedding a site in an iframe to intercept clicks. Protection: X-Frame-Options: DENY, Content Security Policy.

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing):

Browser security mechanism limiting requests to another domain. Configuration: Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods.

Secure Storage:

Cookies: HttpOnly (XSS protection), Secure (HTTPS only), SameSite (CSRF protection). localStorage/sessionStorage: not protected from XSS, store sensitive data only when necessary.

Authentication:

JWT (JSON Web Tokens) — store in memory or HttpOnly cookies. Do not store tokens in localStorage for high-security requirements. Use refresh tokens.

Data Protection:

HTTPS — encrypt all traffic. Password hashing (bcrypt, argon2). Never store passwords in plain text.

Best Practices:

Input validation and sanitization. Principle of least privilege. Regular dependency updates. Use helmet.js (Node.js). Configure CSP. Monitor vulnerabilities (npm audit).
